home.title: Got a suspicious message or call?
home.subtitle: Paste it below and we'll check it for you — links, texts, emails, and phone numbers.
footer.about: About & Privacy — what we store and what we never store
check.privacy: We don't store your uploads or share them with anyone — and we never open the links in your scam.
check.ocr.working: Reading your image — this can take up to a minute the first time. Hang tight…
check.ocr.timeout: OCR is taking too long — try again or paste the text manually.
check.ocr.failed: Couldn't process that image — try a clearer screenshot or paste the text manually.
check.ocr.noText: Couldn't read any text from that image — try a clearer screenshot.
check.shareCopied: Copied — paste it to whoever needs to see it
check.share.summary: Scam check results from Just Checking, Mate:
check.takePhotoDesc: Point at a QR code to decode it
check.uploadEmlDesc: For .eml files exported from a mail app
check.placeholder: Paste a suspicious link, phone number, text message, or email here…
check.pasteHint: On your phone: long-press the message → Copy → tap here → Paste
check.dropHint: On a computer you can also drag a .eml file or screenshot onto this box.
check.forward.heading: On your phone? Forward it to us
check.forward.body: Forward the suspicious email to {address} and we'll email back a plain-English verdict. We read it on arrival and don't keep a copy.
check.forward.note: Forwarding sends the email through your mail provider's servers (unlike pasting, which stays on your device). For anything highly sensitive, paste it instead.
check.serverError: Something went wrong on our end — please try again.
verdict.safe.label: Looks Good
verdict.safe.sub: No red flags found — but always stay cautious.
verdict.suspicious.label: Proceed with Caution
verdict.suspicious.sub: Something feels off. Don't engage until you've verified it.
verdict.suspicious.step1: Don't click any links — look up the contact independently
verdict.suspicious.step2: Don't share personal or financial information
verdict.suspicious.step3: Verify by calling the organisation directly (use a number you found yourself)
verdict.likely_scam.label: Likely a Scam — Do Not Engage
verdict.likely_scam.sub: Don't engage. Block the sender and report it.
verdict.likely_scam.step1: Don't click any links or call any numbers from this message
verdict.likely_scam.step2: Block the sender and delete the message
verdict.likely_scam.step3: If you already shared bank or card details, call your bank now
verdict.unknown.label: Unable to Determine
verdict.unknown.sub: Not enough to go on — trust your instincts.
verdict.overall.heading: Overall verdict
verdict.breakdown.heading: What we checked
tracking.heading: Tracking in this email
tracking.none: No tracking mechanisms were triggered — and because we analysed the source without rendering it, nothing here ever phoned home.
tracking.safe: Opening this in a previewing mail client could have triggered the items above. We analysed the source without rendering it, so none of them fired here.
email.analysis.heading: Email sender analysis
email.analysis.clean: No sender-spoofing signals found in the headers.
phone.heading: Phone details
phone.lineType.premium: Premium rate — costs extra to call
phone.wangiri.title: One-ring scam warning
phone.wangiri.body: Scammers call once and hang up. When you call back you're charged international premium rates. **Do not call back under any circumstances.**
phone.spoof.title: Can scammers fake a phone number?
phone.spoof.body: Yes — scammers can display any number they want as the caller ID. It costs them nothing and is very common. The real source is only known to the phone company and can only be traced by police.
phone.spoof.report.pre: If you've been scammed, report it to
phone.spoof.report.post: and your telco — they can help trace and block the source.
report.urgent: For urgent matters, contact Scamwatch, your bank, or the Australian Federal Police directly — this tool is not a substitute for official reporting channels.
report.valuable: Your report is valuable. Every submission helps raise awareness and protects others from the same scam.
report.stats: **{n}** scams reported by Australians so far
report.placeholder.sms: Paste the full SMS message text...
report.placeholder.email: Paste the email content or the sender's address...
report.placeholder.qr: Paste the URL the QR code resolves to...
report.placeholder.custom: Describe or paste whatever you received...
report.extracted.heading: Extracted from your email
report.extracted.review: Review or edit the fields below before submitting.
report.content.label: The scam content
report.desc.label: What happened?
report.desc.placeholder: E.g. Got a text claiming to be from the ATO asking me to verify my TFN. Almost fell for it...
report.desc.placeholder.custom: Describe what happened — what did you receive, how were you contacted, what did they ask for?
report.ids.hint: Capture any specific contact points so others can recognise the same scam.
report.ids.replyMismatch: Reply-To goes to a different domain than the sender — a classic spoofing sign.
report.email.guide.summary: How do I get the email source?
report.email.guide.intro: Pasting the full source (or a .eml file) lets us read the sender details automatically. Here's how, by app:
report.email.guide.apple.1: Open the email, then choose **View → Message → Raw Source** (or press ⌥⌘U) to see the full source.
report.email.guide.apple.2: Select all (⌘A) and copy (⌘C), then paste it below.
report.email.guide.apple.3: **On iPhone:** drag the email to your Files app, or forward it as an attachment — that gives a .eml you can upload.
report.email.guide.gmail.1: Open the email, click the **⋮** menu (top-right of the message), then **Show original**.
report.email.guide.gmail.2: Click **Copy to clipboard** and paste it below — or **Download Original** to get a .eml file to upload.
report.email.guide.gmail.3: **On mobile:** the app can't show the source — open Gmail in a phone browser, or forward the email to yourself and open it on a computer.
report.email.guide.outlook.1: Double-click the email to open it in its own window, then **File → Properties** — the source is in the **Internet headers** box.
report.email.guide.outlook.2: Select all and copy from that box, then paste it below.
report.email.guide.outlook.3: **Outlook on the web:** open the message, click **⋯ → View → View message source**, then copy it.
report.email.guide.fallback: Can't find it? Just paste the From and Reply-To addresses into the fields above — that's enough to report.
report.email.source.label: Optional: paste full email source or drop a .eml file
report.email.source.placeholder: Paste raw headers here (From:, Reply-To:, ...) — we read them on your device and only submit the addresses, never the full email.
report.email.auth.label: Authentication:
report.parse.notFound: Couldn't find a From or Reply-To header in that — enter the addresses manually below.
report.parse.ok: Pulled out the sender details — review them below before sending.
report.contact.label: Your email
report.contact.hint: Never shared, never published — only used if we need to follow up on your report.
report.error: Something went wrong. Please try again.
report.success.title: Thank you — you've helped protect others.
report.success.body: Your report has been logged. Every submission helps warn others about this scam.
report.success.findIt: Find it later in community reports →
report.success.total: {n} reports submitted by Australians like you.

bug.intro.auto: We hit a problem. With your permission we'll send the details below so we can fix it.
bug.intro.manual: Tell us what's not working. We'll include the details below to help us track it down.
bug.what.label: What happened?
bug.what.placeholder: E.g. I tried to upload a screenshot and it just spun forever.
bug.contact.label: Your email
bug.contact.optional: (optional — only if you want a reply)
bug.privacy: We never include the scam content you pasted or uploaded, or any files — only the details shown above.
bug.error: Couldn't send that just now. Please try again in a moment.
bug.sent.title: Thanks — that helps us improve
bug.sent.body: Your report has been logged. Every one helps us make Just Checking, Mate more reliable.
learn.title: Learn
learn.intro: How scammers work, where scams come from, how to handle one — and what to do if you've already been caught.
learn.types.heading: Common Scam Types We Check
learn.types.1.label: Links & URLs
learn.types.2.label: SMS & texts
learn.types.3.label: Emails
learn.types.4.label: Phone numbers
learn.types.5.label: QR codes
learn.tactics.heading: How Scammers Operate
learn.tactics.intro: Almost every scam leans on the same handful of psychological tricks. Recognise the tactic and the spell breaks.
learn.tactics.1.title: Urgency & fear
learn.tactics.1.desc: “Your account is suspended”, “act within 24 hours” — pressure stops you thinking clearly.
learn.tactics.2.title: Impersonation
learn.tactics.2.desc: They pose as your bank, the ATO, myGov, police, a courier — even a family member (“Hi Mum, my phone broke”).
learn.tactics.3.title: Too good to be true
learn.tactics.3.desc: Surprise prizes, refunds, unexpected inheritances, or crypto/investment returns that can't lose.
learn.tactics.4.title: Borrowed authority
learn.tactics.4.desc: Official logos, spoofed sender names and faked caller ID make a message look legitimate.
learn.tactics.5.title: Unusual payment
learn.tactics.5.desc: Demands for gift cards, crypto, or a transfer to a “safe account” — or asking you to install software.
learn.tactics.6.title: Building rapport
learn.tactics.6.desc: Romance and friendship scams invest weeks earning trust before they ever ask for money.
learn.sources.heading: Where Scams Come From
learn.sources.1.title: Text messages
learn.sources.1.desc: Fake delivery, toll, bank or myGov texts with a link to tap.
learn.sources.2.title: Email
learn.sources.2.desc: Phishing, fake invoices and “verify your account” messages.
learn.sources.3.title: Phone calls
learn.sources.3.desc: Robocalls, “tech support”, and fake bank or government callers.
learn.sources.4.title: Marketplaces & social media
learn.sources.4.desc: Fake sellers and buyers, giveaway and investment ads, hacked friends' accounts.
learn.sources.5.title: Search results & ads
learn.sources.5.desc: Sponsored links and look-alike sites for banks, logins and support numbers.
learn.sources.6.title: Messaging & dating apps
learn.sources.6.desc: WhatsApp “family emergencies”, romance scams, and investment “mentors”.
learn.auth.heading: Email Authentication Checks
learn.auth.intro: When you paste email headers, we automatically check three technical signals that prove whether the sender is who they claim to be. You'll see these badges on community reports too.
learn.auth.spf.desc: Sender Policy Framework — confirms the sending server is on the list of servers authorised to send on behalf of this domain. A fail means the email came from somewhere it shouldn't.
learn.auth.dkim.desc: DomainKeys Identified Mail — a cryptographic signature proving the message wasn't tampered with. Pass is good, but only if the signing domain matches the sender. A pass from an unrelated domain (e.g. a Microsoft tenant) is a red flag.
learn.auth.dmarc.desc: Domain-based Message Authentication — the domain owner's own policy tying SPF and DKIM together. A fail means the message violated the sender's own rules. None means the domain publishes no enforcement — scammers love this.
learn.flags.heading: Common Scam Red Flags
learn.flags.1: Asks for bank, tax file number (TFN) or Medicare details
learn.flags.2: Creates urgent pressure to act right now
learn.flags.3: Promises prizes, refunds or unclaimed money
learn.flags.4: Link doesn't match the organisation it claims to be
learn.flags.6: Asks for payment by gift card or crypto
learn.flags.7: Automated robocall from an unexpected number
learn.flags.8: Email asks you to open a suspicious attachment
learn.handle.heading: If Something Seems Off
learn.handle.1: **Slow down.** Genuine organisations never mind you taking time to check.
learn.handle.2: **Don't engage.** Don't tap links, scan the QR, call the number back, or reply.
learn.handle.3: **Verify independently.** Find the official number or website yourself — never use the contact details in the message.
learn.handle.4: **Never pay unusually.** No gift cards, crypto, or transfers to a “safe account”, and never install apps or grant remote access on request.
learn.handle.5: **Block & delete** the sender once you're sure.
learn.caught.heading: If You've Already Clicked or Shared Details
learn.caught.intro: Don't panic, and don't feel embarrassed — this happens to careful people too. Act quickly, based on what you shared.
learn.caught.1.situation: You clicked a link or entered a login
learn.caught.1.action: Change that password now, and anywhere you reused it. Turn on two-factor authentication and watch the account for activity you don't recognise.
learn.caught.2.situation: You gave bank or card details, or paid
learn.caught.2.action: Call your bank **immediately** (use the number on your card) to freeze cards and stop transfers — recent payments can sometimes be recalled.
learn.caught.3.situation: You shared ID — tax file number, Medicare, licence or passport
learn.caught.3.action: Contact **IDCARE on 1800 595 160** for a free response plan, and consider a credit ban with Equifax, illion and Experian to stop accounts being opened in your name.
learn.caught.4.situation: You installed an app or gave remote access
learn.caught.4.action: Disconnect from the internet, uninstall it, run a security scan, and change important passwords from a different, trusted device.
learn.caught.outro: Then report it to **Scamwatch** and **ReportCyber**, and tell your bank. If you're unsure where to start, IDCARE can walk you through it.
learn.report.heading: Where to report a scam
learn.report.body: Reporting helps authorities warn others and disrupt scammers. This tool gives a best-effort check only — **it can't guarantee it catches every scam**, so trust your instincts too.
